questions.question:
How does the author's use of figurative language help readers understand the explicit and implicit feelings that the narrator is expressing? 

Respond to this question in the form of a paragraph that includes a claim, evidence, and explanation.

responses.content: The author’s use of figurative language help readers understand the explicit and implicit feelings is it can help us understand how Rachel feels about the sweater. For example, Rachel says that the sweater is raggedy and old. We know that Rachel dislikes the sweater. That can be used as explicit feelings. A simile that can be used to know that Rachel dislikes the sweater is, “It's an ugly sweater with red plastic buttons and a collar and sleeves all stretched out like you could use it for a jump rope.” A way of figurative language to show Rachel’s implicit feelings is, “ Only today I wish I didn't have only eleven years rattling inside me like pennies in a tin Band-Aid box.” She says this because she doesn’t know what to say when Mrs. Price put the sweater on Rachel’s desk. Rachel even states that, “I would've known how to tell her it wasn't mine instead of just sitting there with that look on my face and nothing coming out of my mouth.” That is how the author uses figurative language to help readers understand the explicit and implicit feelings that the narrator is expressing.

The author’s use of figurative language help readers understand the explicit and implicit feelings is it can help us understand how Rachel feels about the sweater. For example, Rachel says that the sweater is raggedy and old. We know that Rachel dislikes the sweater. That can be used as explicit feelings. A simile that can be used to know that Rachel dislikes the sweater is, “It's an ugly sweater with red plastic buttons and a collar and sleeves all stretched out like you could use it for a jump rope.” A way of figurative language to show Rachel’s implicit feelings is, “ Only today I wish I didn't have only eleven years rattling inside me like pennies in a tin Band-Aid box.” She says this because she doesn’t know what to say when Mrs. Price put the sweater on Rachel’s desk. Rachel even states that, “I would've known how to tell her it wasn't mine instead of just sitting there with that look on my face and nothing coming out of my mouth.” It helps readers understand more explicit feelings because it directly states it in the text. We already know that Rachel dislikes the sweater. But it would be more difficult to understand how Rachel is feeling. That is called implicit. Implicit means something that is not stated directly in the text. That is how the author uses figurative language to help readers understand the explicit and implicit feelings that the narrator is expressing.

questions.question: Consider how Deza responds to the C+ she received on her essay, both internally and externally. Then imagine how she will approach the next assignment she receives from Mr. Smith. Use the information you learned about both characters, as well as Deza’s other interactions with characters in the text, to write a short scene that describes this event.

responses.content: Deza knew that she had a new essay coming up for Mr. Smith’s class. So Deza decided to study every single day at the library. Deza walked into Mr. Smith’s classroom for a new essay assignment. She sat down, crossed her ankles, and folded her hands on the desk. Deza thought about the strategy that Jimmie once said. She decided to not follow Jimmie’s advice. She gave herself positive affirmations and proved Mr. Smith wrong when she finished the test. The next day, Mr. Smith gave everyone’s paper back. When he got to Deza, she got a big A+! She told Loretta about her grade and Loretta was really impressed! From this day forward, Deza always got good grades. Especially in Mr. Smith’s class.

questions.question:
Consider all the evidence you gathered about how setting influences Brian and Jiang. In one sentence, write a claim that compares and contrasts the role that setting plays in influencing the characters and events in both texts. 

Your claim should address both similarities and differences in how the wilderness setting in “Hatchet” and the school setting in “Red Scarf Girl” affect the characters.

responses.content: The setting affects the characters Brian and Jiang that is different is that Jiang was in a distressed situation in the school grounds about choosing to either choose to save her younger siblings, and is in a social challenge with Thin-Face about her father.  Brian faces a physical challenge by trying to survive in the wilderness and is all alone. The setting affects the characters Brian and Jiang the same is that they both feel trapped and feel like they both can’t do anything about the situation they are in.

questions.question:
“Red Scarf Girl” and “Hatchet” feature young people trapped in challenging situations. In both texts, the setting provides the context for the main conflict or problem. Compare and contrast the role that the setting plays in influencing the characters and events of the two texts. 

Respond to this question in the form of a paragraph that includes a claim, evidence from both texts, and explanation.

responses.content: Brian and Jiang both feel trapped about their situations. One way that their situations are different from each other is that in “Hatchet” it states, “He would probably be home by late night or early morning, home where he could sit down and eat a large, cheesy, juicy burger with tomatoes and double fries with ketchup and a thick chocolate shake.” This explains that Brian was craving a burger back home, trying to distract with the thought of a burger. While Jiang is struggling with her social challenge. “The gray concrete walls closed around me and a slow drizzle dampened my cheeks. I could not go back to the classroom, and I could not go home. I felt like a small animal that had fallen into a trap, alone and helpless, and sure that the hunter was coming.”  A way that their situations are the same is that they both have to find out a way to solve their problems. For example, Jiang needs to make a difficult decision to protect her siblings. Brian needs a way to survive in the wilderness with barely any knowledge at all.

There is no contradiction or fabrication, so 'partial-understanding' is appropriate.

Therefore, it fits best in the 'partial-understanding' category.

questions.question:
How does the author develop the idea that Margaret Bourke-White was a trailblazer? 


Respond to this question in the form of a paragraph that includes your claim, evidence, and explanation.

responses.content: She was a trailblazer because she was the first woman to be a photojournalist. She was also the first woman to get a job at Fortune magazine as a photographer. “In 1929, Henry Luce invited Margaret to work at Fortune magazine. She jumped at the chance and became the first woman in a field called photojournalism, in which photographers reported the news through images.” This piece of evidence proves my claim about her being the first woman photojournalist. Plus, the author uses the words “First” and “Only” like I listed in my previous response. The author also builds on the idea that Margaret is a trailblazer by listing the things she has done like Margaret putting herself into dangerous situations to prove that she is determined and creative. This is how the author develops the idea that Margaret was a trailblazer.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes a clear claim that Margaret Bourke-White was a trailblazer, provides specific evidence from the text (her being the first woman photojournalist and first at Fortune magazine, with a direct quote), and explains how the author develops the idea by using words like “First” and “Only” and by listing her accomplishments in dangerous situations. The explanation connects the evidence to the claim by showing how these actions and the author's word choices demonstrate that Margaret was a trailblazer. The reasoning is present and clear, meeting all requirements for a score of 3.
